edHandoff.summary: 67-year-old female with hx of prior open cholecystectomy (15 years ago) presenting with 2 days of progressive crampy abdominal pain, nausea, bilious vomiting x6, and no BM or flatus for 36 hours. Abdomen distended and tympanitic. CT shows dilated small bowel up to 4.2cm with a transition point in the right lower quadrant and small bowel feces sign. No free air, no wall thickening concerning for ischemia. WBC 9.8, lactate 1.4. NGT placed with 600mL output. Given 2L NS and ondansetron. Pain improved from 8/10 to 5/10 with IV morphine.
edHandoff.disposition: Admit to surgery service for adhesive SBO management. NPO, NGT to suction, serial abdominal exams. Surgery evaluating for trial of non-operative management vs OR.

variants.diagnosis: Partial Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction (Non-Operative Management)

personalityTraits: frustrated about being back in the hospital
patient.communicationStyle: Matter-of-fact about her symptoms. Has been through surgery before and isn't panicked. Frustrated because she thought she was done with surgical problems. Asks reasonable questions about whether she'll need another operation.
patient.emotionalState: Uncomfortable but composed, wants clear answers

responses.opening: My belly started cramping two days ago. At first I thought it was something I ate, but then I started throwing up — green stuff — and I haven't been able to pass gas or have a bowel movement since yesterday morning. The cramps come in waves and my belly is really bloated.
responses.severity: The cramping comes and goes. When it hits, it's maybe a 7 out of 10. Between the waves it's more like a 4. The medicine they gave me here helped some.
responses.onset: Started two days ago. It was mild at first — I thought it was indigestion. But the vomiting started yesterday and hasn't stopped. I've thrown up maybe six or seven times.
responses.vomiting: It's been greenish-yellow. Like bile. The last time there was kind of a foul smell to it. I can't keep anything down, not even water.
responses.bowelFunction: I had a normal bowel movement the morning before this started. Since then, nothing. No gas either. That's what worried me enough to come in.
responses.priorSurgery: I had my gallbladder out about 15 years ago. It was the old-fashioned kind — they cut me open. I have a big scar. No problems since then... until now.
responses.diet: I wasn't eating anything unusual. Normal dinner — chicken and rice. This came out of nowhere.

patientPhrasing: can't pass gas or go to the bathroom
chiefComplaint.onset: Progressive over 2 days. Cramping → bilious vomiting → obstipation (no flatus or stool for 36 hours).
chiefComplaint.timing: Crampy/colicky — comes in waves every 5-10 minutes, lasting 1-2 minutes

present.name: bilious vomiting
present.patientSays: I've been throwing up green/yellow stuff. Maybe 6-7 times.

present.name: obstipation (no flatus or stool)
present.patientSays: Nothing coming out the other end. No gas even. That's what scared me.

present.name: abdominal distension
present.patientSays: I look pregnant. My belly is really swollen.

present.name: crampy/colicky pain
present.patientSays: It comes in waves. Builds up, peaks, then eases off for a few minutes.

present.name: anorexia
present.patientSays: I can't eat. Everything comes right back up.

present.name: mild nausea between cramps
present.patientSays: I feel queasy even between the cramps.

absent.name: bloody stool or hematemesis
absent.patientSays: No blood in the vomit or stool.

absent.name: constant severe pain
absent.patientSays: It's not constant — it comes and goes. Between the cramps I have some relief.

absent.name: diarrhea
absent.patientSays: No, the opposite. Nothing is coming through.

absent.name: urinary symptoms
absent.patientSays: Peeing is fine, maybe a little less than usual.

pmh: Open cholecystectomy (15 years ago — via midline or right subcostal incision)

present: prior open abdominal surgery (adhesions — #1 cause of SBO)

physicalExam.general: Elderly woman in moderate discomfort, lying still between cramps, guarding during pain waves. Well-nourished.

abdominal.inspection: Distended. Well-healed right subcostal surgical scar (cholecystectomy). No visible hernia.
abdominal.auscultation: High-pitched, hyperactive bowel sounds with intermittent rushes (coincide with cramping). Occasional tinkling sounds.
abdominal.palpation: Diffuse mild tenderness without focal peritoneal signs. No rebound tenderness. No rigidity. No palpable masses. No inguinal or femoral hernias bilaterally.

physicalExam.rectal: Empty rectal vault. No masses. Guaiac negative.

labs.note: Normal — no leukocytosis argues against strangulation/ischemia
Hypokalemia + hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis = classic electrolyte pattern from vomiting (loss of HCl). Prerenal azotemia from dehydration.

labs.value: Concentrated, SG 1.030, no WBC, no bacteria

imaging.findings: Dilated small bowel loops up to 4.2cm proximal to a transition point in the right lower quadrant. Decompressed distal small bowel and colon. Small bowel feces sign at the transition point. No wall thickening, pneumatosis, or portal venous gas. No free air. No closed-loop configuration. Trace free fluid in pelvis (physiologic amount). Adhesive band suspected at prior surgical site.
imaging.impression: High-grade partial small bowel obstruction, most likely adhesive. Transition point in RLQ. No evidence of strangulation, ischemia, or closed-loop obstruction.

imaging.study: Abdominal X-ray (KUB — obtained in ED before CT)
imaging.findings: Multiple dilated small bowel loops with air-fluid levels. Paucity of colonic gas. No free air.

admittingOrders.ngt: NGT to low intermittent suction. Document output every shift. Current output: 600mL bilious.
admittingOrders.ivFluids: NS with 20mEq KCl at 150mL/hr. Replace additional losses mL-for-mL with NS + KCl based on NGT output. Goal: UOP >0.5mL/kg/hr. Replete K to >4.0.
admittingOrders.painManagement: Hydromorphone 0.5mg IV q4h PRN. Avoid NSAIDs (renal risk with dehydration). Acetaminophen 1g IV q6h scheduled.
admittingOrders.antiemetics: Ondansetron 4mg IV q6h PRN (though NGT should address most nausea).
admittingOrders.monitoring: Vitals q4h. Strict I&O (NGT output + urine output). BMP in AM (trend electrolytes, especially K and bicarb). Abdominal exam every shift by surgical team — document distension, tenderness, bowel sounds.
admittingOrders.serialExams: CRITICAL — check abdomen q6-8h for signs of deterioration: increasing tenderness, peritoneal signs (rebound, rigidity), fever, tachycardia, rising WBC or lactate = operative indication.
admittingOrders.waterSolubleContrast: Consider Gastrografin challenge (90mL via NGT, clamp x4h, repeat KUB at 4-8h). Contrast reaching colon by 4-8 hours predicts successful non-operative management with 97% sensitivity. Also has therapeutic osmotic effect.
admittingOrders.dvtProphylaxis: Enoxaparin 40mg SQ daily (surgical patients at high risk for DVT during immobility).
admittingOrders.surgicalPlan: Trial of non-operative management for 48-72 hours (adhesive SBO without signs of strangulation). Clear criteria for OR: peritoneal signs, clinical deterioration, fever, rising WBC/lactate, failure to improve in 48-72 hours, or closed-loop obstruction.

onCorrectTreatment.vitalsTrend: Stable. HR normalizes to 78. NGT output decreasing from 600mL → 200mL → 100mL over 48 hours.
onCorrectTreatment.symptomsTrend: Cramping diminishing. First flatus at 36 hours. Distension improving. Bowel sounds normalizing.
onCorrectTreatment.narrative: NGT decompression, IV fluids, and bowel rest for 48 hours. Gastrografin reaches colon by 6 hours on repeat KUB — predicts resolution. NGT output decreasing. Patient passes flatus on day 2. Diet advanced from clear liquids → full liquids → low-residue diet. NGT removed. Discharged on day 3-4 with bowel regimen and surgery follow-up.

onFailedConservative.timeline: If no improvement by 48-72 hours, or if any signs of deterioration at any point:
onFailedConservative.narrative: Persistent high NGT output (>500mL/day), no flatus, worsening distension → operative management. Exploratory laparotomy with lysis of adhesions. If bowel viable → simple lysis. If bowel non-viable → resection with primary anastomosis or diverting ostomy.

keyLearningPoints: The classic SBO triad: colicky abdominal pain + bilious vomiting + obstipation (no flatus or stool)
Adhesions from prior surgery are the #1 cause of SBO (65-75%). Open surgery causes more adhesions than laparoscopic.
CT with IV contrast is the imaging modality of choice: identifies transition point, assesses for strangulation, excludes other pathology
Small bowel feces sign = particulate matter in dilated small bowel proximal to obstruction — pathognomonic for SBO
Hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis is the classic electrolyte pattern from prolonged vomiting (loss of gastric HCl)
Non-operative management (NGT, NPO, IV fluids) is appropriate when: partial obstruction, no signs of strangulation, no closed-loop
Gastrografin challenge is both diagnostic (contrast in colon by 4-8h = will resolve) and therapeutic (osmotic effect)
Serial abdominal exams are the most important monitoring tool — more important than labs
mustNotMiss: Strangulated obstruction (constant severe pain, peritoneal signs, fever, elevated lactate, elevated WBC — SURGICAL EMERGENCY)
Closed-loop obstruction on CT (C-shaped or U-shaped loop with 2 transition points — high strangulation risk, often needs OR)
Incarcerated hernia (always check inguinal, femoral, and incisional hernia sites — treatable cause)
SBO from malignancy (new diagnosis SBO without prior surgery → concern for obstructing tumor, especially in older patients)
Pneumatosis or portal venous gas on CT (bowel ischemia/necrosis — emergent OR)
commonPitfalls: Ordering a CT without IV contrast — you need contrast to assess bowel wall enhancement and ischemia
Not checking hernia orifices — incarcerated inguinal or femoral hernia is a correctable cause that's missed if you don't examine
Using opioids excessively — they slow bowel motility. Use minimum effective dose.
Ignoring electrolyte losses from NGT drainage — replace NGT output mL-for-mL with NS + KCl
Not documenting serial abdominal exams — if the patient goes to the OR, the progression (or lack thereof) guides timing
Waiting too long for non-operative management to work in a patient who is clearly deteriorating — the 48-72 hour window assumes the patient is STABLE or IMPROVING
teaching.attendingQuestions: Your patient has a partial adhesive SBO. Walk me through your non-operative management plan. What are you monitoring, how often, and what are your hard indications for the OR? Also — her K is 3.3 and bicarb is 30. Why? What's causing that electrolyte pattern?

variants.diagnosis: Complete Small Bowel Obstruction with Strangulation

patient.communicationStyle: In too much pain for extended conversation. Answers are brief and pained. The pain is CONSTANT — not colicky. This shift from colicky to constant pain is a critical teaching point.

responses.opening: Something is really wrong... the pain used to come and go but now it's constant and it's the worst pain of my life. My stomach is rock hard. I feel like I'm going to die.
responses.painCharacter: It used to cramp... but now it doesn't let up. It's constant. Everything hurts. Especially right here (points to RLQ/periumbilical). Even light touch on my belly is agony.
responses.vomiting: I've been throwing up for two days. Now it smells terrible — like sewage. I can't stop.

chiefComplaint.onset: Initially colicky (2 days), transitioned to CONSTANT severe pain 6 hours ago — this shift indicates strangulation/ischemia
chiefComplaint.severity: 10/10 constant. No relief with any position or medication.
chiefComplaint.keyChange: Transition from colicky → constant pain = bowel compromise. This is the critical inflection point.

present.name: constant severe abdominal pain (no longer colicky)
present.patientSays: It doesn't let up anymore. It used to come in waves.

present.name: feculent vomiting
present.patientSays: The vomit smells like... feces. It's horrible.

pmh: Prior small bowel obstruction (managed non-operatively 5 years ago)

present: prior SBO episode (recurrence increases strangulation risk)
transition from colicky to constant pain

physicalExam.general: Elderly male in severe distress, writhing, diaphoretic, tachycardic

abdominal.inspection: Markedly distended. Old RLQ appendectomy scar. Tense.
abdominal.auscultation: Absent bowel sounds (ileus from ischemia — previously had hyperactive sounds).
abdominal.palpation: DIFFUSE TENDERNESS WITH INVOLUNTARY GUARDING AND REBOUND TENDERNESS. Peritoneal signs present. Rigid abdomen.

labs.note: Markedly elevated — bowel ischemia/strangulation. This is an OPERATIVE indicator.

imaging.findings: Dilated small bowel up to 5.0cm. Closed-loop configuration in the RLQ — C-shaped loop with two adjacent transition points at an adhesive band. Bowel wall thickening and decreased enhancement of the affected segment (ischemic loop). Mesenteric haziness and engorgement of mesenteric vessels. Moderate free fluid. No pneumatosis or portal venous gas yet.
imaging.impression: Closed-loop small bowel obstruction with signs of strangulation. EMERGENT SURGICAL INTERVENTION REQUIRED.

immediateActions: This is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Call attending surgeon STAT for OR.
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation: LR wide open (2L bolus, then titrate to hemodynamics).
Broad-spectrum antibiotics: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV (cover enteric organisms for potential bowel perforation/translocation).
Type and screen / crossmatch 2 units PRBC.
Central line and arterial line in OR if hemodynamically unstable.
managementPlan.operativeApproach: Exploratory laparotomy. Lyse adhesive band causing closed-loop. Assess bowel viability (color, peristalsis, mesenteric pulsations). If viable → release and observe. If non-viable → resection with primary anastomosis (if patient stable and clean field) or resection with diverting ileostomy + mucous fistula (if contaminated or unstable).

keyLearningPoints: The transition from COLICKY to CONSTANT pain is the single most important clinical indicator of bowel strangulation
Strangulation signs: constant pain, peritoneal signs, fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, elevated lactate, hemodynamic instability
Closed-loop obstruction (two adjacent transition points creating a sealed loop) has the highest risk of strangulation — often requires emergent surgery
Absent bowel sounds in SBO = ileus from ischemia (previously hyperactive sounds become silent as bowel dies)
Feculent vomiting indicates complete distal obstruction with bacterial overgrowth in stagnant proximal bowel
Elevated lactate in SBO = bowel ischemia until proven otherwise — this is an OPERATIVE indication
Do NOT delay surgery for 'optimization' when strangulation is suspected — source control is the treatment
mustNotMiss: Bowel perforation (free air on imaging or Pneumoperitoneum)
Mesenteric ischemia without mechanical obstruction (older patient, atrial fibrillation, atherosclerosis)
Volvulus (sigmoid or cecal — different surgical approach)
teaching.attendingQuestions: Your SBO patient from yesterday — the one who was doing okay on conservative management — now has constant pain, a rigid abdomen, WBC of 22, and lactate of 5.8. What changed? What's your differential for the change, and what's the plan right now? You have about 15 minutes before the OR opens up — what do you need to do in that window?
